title: An implemented model of punning riddles
research_questions: Can a formal symbolic model generate simple question-answer word-substitution punning riddles from a humour-independent lexicon?
Can separating schemata for lexical/semantic relations from templates for surface form account for the selected subtype of punning riddles?
How recognizable and good are JAPE-1's generated riddles when judged by volunteers?

methods_proposed.name: JAPE-1
methods_proposed.summary: A computer program that generates a restricted subtype of punning riddle: homonym-substitution riddles with noun phrase punchlines. It uses a humour-independent lexicon, a homonym base, six schemata, templates, and simple post-production checks.

methods_proposed.name: Schema-template model of punning riddles
methods_proposed.summary: Schemata specify the lexical and semantic relations among a real phrase, a homonymous substituted word, the fake phrase, and the constructed meaning. Templates then map an instantiated schema into a question-answer joke surface form.

methods_proposed.name: Post-production checker
methods_proposed.summary: JAPE-1 applies simple checks to reject outputs where lexemes used in the question and punchline are accidentally identical, or where the lexemes used for the nonsense noun phrase and its meaning form a genuine common noun phrase.

humor_theories.detail: The paper treats puns as exploiting phonological ambiguity, where the riddlee is asked to accept phonological, morphological, or syntactic similarity as semantic comparison; JAPE-1 operationalizes this through homonym word substitution in schemata and templates.

evaluation_methods.detail: JAPE-1 produced 188 jokes in near-surface form, distributed in batches to 14 judges, who scored them from 0 (“Not a joke. Doesn’t make any sense.”) to 5 (“Really good”) and also gave qualitative comments.
During the evaluation procedure, a “common knowledge judge” checked volunteer-written lexical entries for errors and excessively obscure suggestions before they were entered into JAPE-1’s lexicon.

headline_results.finding: JAPE-1 produced a set of jokes for evaluation from volunteer-provided lexical data.
Across all jokes produced from the volunteer lexical data, the average score was low.
The highest-scoring outputs showed strong judge disagreement: every joke receiving a 5 from one judge received a low score from the other judge.
headline_results.value: nine jokes; three got zeroes, three got ones, and three got twos

headline_results.finding: For the “pool queue” example, a more appropriate template produced a joke that judges rated better than the non-joke output from the less appropriate template.
An output produced from an overly general lexical definition, “What kind of device has wings? An aeroplane hanger,” scored very poorly.

key_findings: JAPE-1 can generate pieces of text that are recognizably jokes from a relatively unbiased, humour-independent lexicon.
The generated jokes were generally weak: the average score was 1.5 points on a 0–5 scale, and the paper characterizes the output as “jokes, but pathetic ones.”
Schemata and templates varied in quality, with some template choices producing non-jokes and more appropriate templates producing better-rated variants.
Lexical entry quality mattered: volunteer definitions that were too general produced poor jokes such as the “aeroplane hanger” example.
The paper argues that improving lexical precision, adding lexical slots, refining templates, removing poor schema-template links, and extending to spoonerisms and sub-word puns could improve JAPE-1.
limitations: The evaluation was explicitly not statistically rigorous.
The paper states that there were too few jokes and joke judges for the scores to be statistically significant.
There was no control group of human-produced jokes judged under the same conditions.
JAPE-1 is restricted to riddles that use word substitution, put the substituted word in the punchline, substitute a homonym, and use a common noun phrase punchline.
The authors did not have access to a suitable large lexicon and relied on volunteer-written lexical entries.
Volunteer definitions were often too general for JAPE-1’s purposes.
The homonym base was shortened considerably and contains only common, concrete nouns and adjectives.

human_samples.observations: They were asked to make lexical entries for a set of words; the number of volunteers, words, and lexical entries is not reported.
human_samples.detail: These volunteer-written entries were used to build the lexicon for the evaluation so that the authors did not hand-craft humour-oriented lexical data.

human_samples.observations: Sifted the volunteer-written lexical entries for errors and excessively obscure suggestions before entry into JAPE-1’s lexicon; the number checked, changed, or rejected is not reported.
human_samples.detail: The paper describes this as the second stage of the informal evaluation procedure, after data acquisition and before joke judging.

human_samples.observations: 188 JAPE-1 jokes in near-surface form were distributed in batches; judges scored jokes from 0 to 5 and gave qualitative comments such as possible improvements and whether they had heard a joke before. The paper does not fully specify the per-joke assignment design, though it refers to an “other judge” for jokes that received a maximum score from one judge.
human_samples.detail: These volunteers provided the primary joke-quality evidence reported in the paper.

judges.name: Common knowledge judge
judges.detail: Checked volunteer-supplied lexical entries for errors and excessively obscure suggestions before those entries were used in the JAPE-1 lexicon. Expertise, instructions beyond this check, and blinding are not reported.

judges.name: 14 volunteer joke judges
judges.detail: Scored generated jokes on a 0–5 scale where 0 meant “Not a joke. Doesn’t make any sense.” and 5 meant “Really good”; also provided qualitative information. Demographics, recruitment, blinding, batch sizes, and exact per-joke rating counts are not reported.

generation_selection.scope: JAPE-1 generation for the informal volunteer evaluation
generation_selection.detail: JAPE-1 produced a set of 188 jokes in near-surface form from volunteer-provided lexical data. The program used schemata, templates, and a post-production checker that rejected outputs where lexemes in the question and punchline were accidentally identical or where the lexemes used for the nonsense noun phrase and its meaning formed a genuine common noun phrase. The paper presents the 188 as the jokes produced from those lexical data and does not report best-of-N sampling, ranking, or human cherry-picking.

model_provenance.name: JAPE-1
model_provenance.detail: Implemented symbolic joke generator described as the current version of JAPE-1; exact implementation version, run date, execution environment, and code access are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 188 jokes total; no item-level prompts or per-item candidate counts are reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports that JAPE-1 had six schemata, a set of templates, a homonym base, and post-production checks. It does not report the number of source words, number of lexical entries, pre-filter candidate count, rejection count, runtime, compute, or cost.

human_baselines.description: No control group of human-produced jokes was judged under the same conditions. The authors explicitly state that they do not know how human-produced jokes of this genre would fare if judged in the same way, and describe a mixed human/JAPE evaluation only as an ideal hindsight design.
human_baselines.detail: The Crack-a-Joke Book was studied as source material and some existing jokes are discussed, but no matched human baseline was included in the reported evaluation.

mitigations: Volunteer lexical-entry writers were used instead of author hand-crafted entries to reduce humour-oriented bias in the lexicon.
A common knowledge judge checked volunteer entries for errors and excessive obscurity.
Joke judges were asked whether they had heard any of the jokes before.
contamination_risk.basis: There is no fixed public benchmark or learned model training set in the modern contamination sense; JAPE-1 is a symbolic generator and the evaluated set was generated from volunteer-supplied lexical data, then rated by humans. However, the authors developed the model after studying existing riddles and report that some high-end outputs already existed as jokes, so novelty overlap is possible and was not fully controlled or quantified.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Primary scores came from volunteer human joke judges; no LLM judge was used.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The paper predates LLM-based evaluation and reports no automatic or LLM evaluator for joke quality.

reporting_gaps: Number, demographics, recruitment method, and geography of the lexical-entry volunteers are not reported.
The number of source words, number of volunteer-written lexical entries, and number of entries accepted, edited, or rejected by the common knowledge judge are not reported.
The common knowledge judge’s identity, expertise, detailed criteria, and blinding are not reported.
Joke-judge demographics, recruitment method, geography, and prior familiarity with the task are not reported.
Batch sizes, randomization, blinding, instructions beyond the rating scale and qualitative prompts, and the exact number of ratings per joke are not fully specified.
The evaluation is explicitly described as informal and not statistically rigorous; no statistical significance testing is provided.
No matched human-produced joke control group was included, which the paper explicitly notes.
The exact implementation version, run date, code availability, execution environment, runtime, compute, and cost are not reported.

Generation budget details are missing: pre-filter candidate count, rejection count from post-production checking, schema/template usage counts, and any ordering or sampling procedure are not reported.
The size of the shortened homonym base and the detailed criteria used to shorten it are not reported.
The number of outputs that judges identified as pre-existing jokes is not quantified beyond listed examples.
